36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.

41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65

67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.

96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).

131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.

The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.

Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).

To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101

56	/-- **Cartesian-monoidal identity (skeleton step of the Rigidity Lemma).** Post-composing the
57	second projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y` with the slice section `y ↦ (x₀, y)` is the "collapse the
58	`X`-axis onto `x₀`" endomorphism `(x, y) ↦ (x₀, y)` of `X ⊗ Y`:
59	`snd ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) = lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`.
60	
61	Pure cartesian-monoidal algebra (no geometry): `comp_lift` distributes the `snd`, the
62	`𝟙 Y` component simplifies by `Category.comp_id`, and the `toUnit Y` component collapses by
63	uniqueness of maps into the terminal object. -/
64	theorem rigidity_snd_lift
65	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
66	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X) :
67	    snd X Y ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) =
68	      lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) := by
69	  ext1 <;> simp

71	/-- **Bridge 1 of the Rigidity Lemma (closed-map step), PROVEN.** When `X` is complete (proper)
72	over `k̄`, the second monoidal projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y` has, on underlying schemes, a
73	*closed* base map. This is Mumford's "completeness of `X` makes `p₂` a closed map" (Abelian
74	Varieties, Ch. II §4, p. 43).
75	
76	Proof: the underlying scheme morphism `(snd X Y).left` is the pullback projection
77	`Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` (`Over.snd_left`), i.e. the base change of `X.hom` along
78	`Y.hom`. `IsProper X.hom ⟹ UniversallyClosed X.hom` (`IsProper.toUniversallyClosed`), and
79	`UniversallyClosed` is stable under base change
80	(`universallyClosed_isStableUnderBaseChange.of_isPullback` on the canonical pullback square), so
81	`(snd X Y).left` is universally closed and hence its base map is closed
82	(`Scheme.Hom.isClosedMap`). Char-free; no theorem of the cube, no cohomology. -/
83	theorem snd_left_isClosedMap
84	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper X.hom] :
85	    IsClosedMap (snd X Y).left.base := by
86	  haveI hp : UniversallyClosed X.hom := IsProper.toUniversallyClosed
87	  haveI : UniversallyClosed (snd X Y).left := by
88	    rw [Over.snd_left]
89	    exact universallyClosed_isStableUnderBaseChange.of_isPullback
90	      (IsPullback.of_hasPullback X.hom Y.hom) hp
91	  exact Scheme.Hom.isClosedMap _
92	
93	/-- **The dense-open agreement (the genuine geometric content, deferred).** Mumford's open
94	`X × V` together with the slice-constancy `f(x, y) = f(x₀, y)` on it, packaged as the single
95	existential that `rigidity_core`'s gluing step consumes: there is a non-empty open `U` of
96	`(X ⊗ Y).left` on which `f` and the collapsed map `retract ≫ f` agree as scheme morphisms.
97	
98	This lemma carries the **collapse hypothesis** `_hf : f(X × {y₀}) = {z₀}` (encoded
99	`lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀`), which is exactly what makes Mumford's open
100	`V := Y ∖ G` non-empty (`y₀ ∉ G`, since the rigidified slice `f(X × {y₀}) = {z₀} ⊆ U` lands in
101	the affine `U`, so its image under `snd` avoids `G = snd '' (f ⁻¹ F)`). Without `_hf` the lemma
102	is **false** (e.g. `f := fst : X ⊗ Y ⟶ X = Z` has no open of agreement). The full instance set
103	(`GeometricallyIrreducible`, `IsReduced`, `IsSeparated`) and `_hf` only strengthen the
104	antecedent.

106	This is where the two char-free Mathlib bridges of `rigidity_core`'s docstring are discharged:
107	the closed-map argument (`IsProper.toUniversallyClosed` ⟹ the projection is closed) produces the
108	non-empty open `U = X × V`, and the affine-constancy argument
109	(`isField_of_universallyClosed` on each proper integral slice mapping to an affine) supplies the
110	scheme-level equality on `U`. It is the sole remaining `sorry` of the Rigidity-Lemma chain. -/
111	theorem rigidity_eqOn_dense_open
112	    {X Y Z : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
113	    [IsProper X.hom]
114	    [GeometricallyIrreducible (X ⊗ Y).hom]
115	    [IsReduced (X ⊗ Y).left]
116	    [IsSeparated Z.hom]
117	    (f : (X ⊗ Y) ⟶ Z)
118	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X)
119	    (y₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Y)
120	    (z₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Z)
121	    (_hf : lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀) :
122	    ∃ U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens, (U : Set (X ⊗ Y).left).Nonempty ∧
123	      (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) ≫ f.left =
124	        (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) ≫
125	          (lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f).left := by
126	  -- Mumford's construction of the open `U = X × V`, `V = Y - G`, `G = p₂(f⁻¹(Z - U₀))`.
127	  -- Bridge 1 (`snd_left_isClosedMap`) makes `G` closed; the collapse hypothesis `_hf` makes
128	  -- `y₀ ∉ G` so `V` (hence `U`) is non-empty; bridge 2 (affine-constancy) is the agreement.
129	  have hclosed : IsClosedMap (snd X Y).left.base := snd_left_isClosedMap
130	  -- `Spec k̄` is a single point; transport the instance to the monoidal unit's underlying scheme.
131	  haveI hsub : Subsingleton (↥(𝟙_ (Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)))).left) :=
132	    inferInstanceAs (Subsingleton (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)))
133	  -- The chosen `k̄`-point of `Spec k̄` (`= (𝟙_).left`).
134	  have ptk : (𝟙_ (Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)))).left :=
135	    (inferInstance : Inhabited (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar))).default
136	  -- The image point `z₀` in `Z` and an affine open neighbourhood `U₀ ∋ z₀`.
137	  let z₀pt : Z.left := z₀.left.base ptk
138	  obtain ⟨U₀, _hU₀aff, hz₀U₀, -⟩ := exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := Z.left)
139	    (x := z₀pt) (U := ⊤) trivial
140	  -- `G = p₂(f⁻¹(Z - U₀))` is closed (image of a closed set under the closed map `p₂`).
141	  set Gset := (snd X Y).left.base '' (f.left.base ⁻¹' (U₀ : Set Z.left)ᶜ) with hGdef
142	  have hG : IsClosed Gset := hclosed _ (U₀.isOpen.isClosed_compl.preimage f.left.base.hom.2)

144	  have hUopen : IsOpen ((snd X Y).left.base ⁻¹' Gsetᶜ) :=
145	    (hG.isOpen_compl).preimage (snd X Y).left.base.hom.2
146	  -- The slice section `s : X → X ⊗ Y`, `x ↦ (x, y₀)`, and the points `y₀`, `x₀`.
147	  let s := (lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀)).left
148	  let y₀pt : Y.left := y₀.left.base ptk
149	  let x₀pt : X.left := x₀.left.base ptk
150	  -- GAP (pullback-fibre topology, isolated): every point of the slice `p₂⁻¹{y₀}` lies in the
151	  -- image of the section `s`. True because `y₀` is a `k̄`-point: the scheme fibre over it is
152	  -- `X ×_{Spec k̄} Spec k̄ = X`, and `s` is the canonical identification of `X` with that fibre.
153	  -- (Needs the range-of-pullback-projection-fibre lemma; see task_results for the precise gap.)
154	  have hfib : (snd X Y).left.base ⁻¹' {y₀pt} ⊆ Set.range s.base := sorry
155	  -- `y₀ ∉ G`: any point over `y₀` is `s x`, and `_hf` collapses `f (s x) = z₀ ∈ U₀`.
156	  have hy₀ : y₀pt ∉ Gset := by
157	    rintro ⟨q, hq, hsndq⟩
158	    obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := hfib (by simpa using hsndq)
159	    apply hq
160	    have hcomp : s ≫ f.left = (toUnit X ≫ z₀).left := by
161	      rw [← Over.comp_left]; exact congrArg Over.Hom.left _hf
162	    have hfx : f.left.base (s.base x) = z₀pt := by
163	      rw [← Scheme.Hom.comp_apply, hcomp, Over.comp_left, Scheme.Hom.comp_apply]
164	      change z₀.left.base ((toUnit X).left.base x) = z₀.left.base ptk
165	      congr 1; exact Subsingleton.elim _ _
166	    rw [hfx]; exact hz₀U₀
167	  -- Assemble `U`, witness its non-emptiness by `s x₀` (which lies over `y₀ ∈ V`).
168	  refine ⟨⟨_, hUopen⟩, ⟨s.base x₀pt, ?_⟩, ?_⟩
169	  · change (snd X Y).left.base (s.base x₀pt) ∈ Gsetᶜ
170	    have hsnd : (snd X Y).left.base (s.base x₀pt) = y₀pt := by
171	      have hcomp : s ≫ (snd X Y).left = (toUnit X ≫ y₀).left := by
172	        rw [← Over.comp_left]; exact congrArg Over.Hom.left (lift_snd (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀))
173	      rw [← Scheme.Hom.comp_apply, hcomp, Over.comp_left, Scheme.Hom.comp_apply]
174	      change y₀.left.base ((toUnit X).left.base x₀pt) = y₀.left.base ptk
175	      congr 1; exact Subsingleton.elim _ _
176	    rw [Set.mem_compl_iff, hsnd]; exact hy₀
177	  · -- Bridge 2 (affine-constancy): on `U = X × V` each proper slice `X × {y}` maps into the
178	    -- affine `U₀`, hence to a single point `f(x₀, y)`, so `f` and `retract ≫ f` agree on `U`.
179	    -- This is the scheme-morphism equality that needs the relative "proper-into-affine is
180	    -- constant" / `O_X = k̄`-pushforward bridge; isolated as the residual geometric input.
181	    sorry
182	
183	/-- **Geometric core of the Rigidity Lemma (PROVEN, modulo `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`).** With
184	`X` complete (proper) and `x₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ X` a `k̄`-point, the morphism `f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` is invariant
185	under the "collapse-the-`X`-axis-onto-`x₀`" endomorphism
186	`retract := lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`:
187	
188	  `f = retract ≫ f`,
189	
190	i.e. `f(x, y) = f(x₀, y)` for all `(x, y)` — `f` depends only on the `Y`-coordinate. This is
191	the entirety of the *geometric* content of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma (Form I); the rest of
192	`rigidity_lemma` is the cartesian-monoidal algebra discharged by `rigidity_snd_lift`.
193	
194	This core is now **proven** by the project's scheme-level rigidity (replicated inline from
195	Mathlib's `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`, since the wrapper `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen`
196	lives in the downstream `AlgebraicJacobian.Rigidity`): two maps out of the geometrically
197	irreducible reduced `X ⊗ Y` into the separated `Z` that agree on a non-empty open agree
198	everywhere. The single deferred input is the existence of that non-empty open together with the
199	agreement on it — `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` — which is the actual geometry below.
200	
201	This core carries the **collapse hypothesis** `_hf : f(X × {y₀}) = {z₀}` (encoded
202	`lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀`), passed straight through to
203	`rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`: it is exactly what makes Mumford's open `V := Y ∖ G` non-empty
204	(`y₀ ∉ G`). Without it the core statement is **false** (`f := fst` is not collapse-invariant).
205	
206	## Mumford's proof of `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` (Abelian Varieties, Ch. II §4, p. 43)
207	
208	Set `g(y) = f(x₀, y)` (so `retract ≫ f` is exactly `(x, y) ↦ g(y)`). The open `V` is produced as
209	follows: let `U` be an affine open neighbourhood of a chosen point in `Z`, `F = Z ∖ U`, and
210	`G = (snd X Y) '' (f ⁻¹ F)`. Then for each `y ∈ V := Y ∖ G`, the slice `f(X × {y}) ⊆ U`, and `f`
211	agrees with `retract ≫ f` on the non-empty open `X × V`.
212	
213	This rests on **two Mathlib bridges still to be built** (the cube-free heart):
214	
215	  1. **Properness ⇒ the projection is a closed map.** `IsProper X.hom` is universally closed
216	     (`AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper.toUniversallyClosed`), and `snd X Y` is the base change of
217	     `X.hom` along `Y.hom`, hence universally closed, hence a closed map
218	     (`AlgebraicGeometry.UniversallyClosed.universally_isClosedMap` instantiated at the relevant
219	     pullback square). So `G = snd '' (closed)` is closed and `V` is open. The missing glue is
220	     identifying the monoidal `snd X Y` in `Over (Spec k̄)` with the scheme-theoretic pullback
221	     projection `Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` and transporting `IsClosedMap` across that
222	     identification. (Search: `MorphismProperty.pullback`, `Over.tensor`/`Over.cartesian…`,
223	     `Limits.pullback.snd`.)
224	
225	  2. **A proper connected variety mapping to an affine has image a single point.** For `y ∈ V`,
226	     the proper connected slice `X × {y}` maps under `f` into the affine `U`; a global regular
227	     function on a proper integral `k̄`-scheme is constant. Mathlib *has* the key fact:
228	     `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed` — for `X` integral and `f : X ⟶ Spec K`
229	     universally closed (`K` a field), `Γ(X, ⊤)` is a field. Combined with `Γ(affine U) → Γ(X×{y})`
230	     being a `k̄`-algebra map into a field that is finite over `k̄`
231	     (`AlgebraicGeometry.finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` under `LocallyOfFiniteType`) and
232	     `k̄` algebraically closed, the map `X × {y} → U` factors through a single point. This is the
233	     "global-sections-constant" argument; assembling it into "image is one point" is the
234	     remaining work.
235	
236	These are exactly the "two Mathlib bridges to find/build" flagged in `PROGRESS.md`. iter-157
237	located concrete Mathlib entry points for *both* (named above), upgrading the prior "likely
238	lacks this" assessment: the obstruction is assembly + the monoidal-`snd`-as-pullback
239	identification, not absent infrastructure. Both bridges are char-free (no theorem of the cube,
240	no cohomology beyond `H⁰`). Until they are assembled, `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` is the sole
241	`sorry` of the Rigidity-Lemma chain — both the categorical reduction (`rigidity_lemma`,
242	`rigidity_snd_lift`) and the scheme-level gluing (`rigidity_core`) are closed. -/
243	theorem rigidity_core
244	    {X Y Z : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
245	    [IsProper X.hom]
246	    [GeometricallyIrreducible (X ⊗ Y).hom]
247	    [IsReduced (X ⊗ Y).left]
248	    [IsSeparated Z.hom]
249	    (f : (X ⊗ Y) ⟶ Z)
250	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X)
251	    (y₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Y)
252	    (z₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Z)
253	    (_hf : lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀) :
254	    f = lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f := by
255	  -- The gluing step is scheme-level rigidity: two maps out of a geometrically irreducible reduced
256	  -- source into a separated target that agree on a non-empty open agree everywhere. We replicate
257	  -- the argument of `AlgebraicJacobian.Rigidity`'s `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` inline (that
258	  -- file is *downstream* of this one, so its wrapper is unavailable here) directly from Mathlib's
259	  -- `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`. The non-empty open and the agreement on it are the
260	  -- genuine geometric content, isolated in `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`.
261	  obtain ⟨U, hU, h⟩ := rigidity_eqOn_dense_open f x₀ y₀ z₀ _hf
262	  -- `Spec k̄` is a single point, so geometric irreducibility makes `(X ⊗ Y).left` irreducible.
263	  haveI : IrreducibleSpace (X ⊗ Y).left :=
264	    GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace_of_subsingleton (X ⊗ Y).hom
265	  -- A non-empty open of an irreducible space is dense, so its inclusion is dominant.
266	  haveI : IsDominant (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) :=
267	    Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant_ι (IsOpen.dense U.isOpen hU)
268	  -- Provide separatedness of `Z.left` over `Spec k̄` in the `OverClass.fromOver` form.
269	  haveI : IsSeparated (Z.left ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)) := ‹IsSeparated Z.hom›
270	  -- Promote the underlying-scheme equality to an `Over (Spec k̄)` equality.
271	  refine Over.OverMorphism.ext ?_
272	  exact ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated' (S := Spec (.of kbar))
273	    (X := (X ⊗ Y).left) (Y := Z.left) (f := f.left)
274	    (g := (lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f).left) U.ι h
275	
276	/-- **Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I).** Let `X` be a complete (proper) variety and `Y`, `Z`
277	any varieties over `k̄`. If `f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` collapses the slice `X × {y₀}` to a single point
278	`z₀` (encoded as `lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀`), then `f` factors through
279	the second projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y`, i.e. there is `g : Y ⟶ Z` with `f = snd ≫ g`.
280	
281	This is the cube-free, cohomology-free entry point of the chain: its only inputs are that
282	completeness of `X` makes the projection a closed map, and that a proper connected variety has
283	no nonconstant map to an affine variety. Valid in arbitrary characteristic.

288	## iter-157 signature correction (the scaffold was false as stated)
289	
290	The iter-157 scaffold carried only `[IsProper X.hom]`. **That statement is false**: take `X` =
291	two disjoint reduced `k̄`-points (proper but disconnected), `Y` = two points, `Z = X`, and
292	`f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` collapsing the `y₀`-fibre to one point while separating the other fibre. Then
293	`_hf` holds yet `f` does not factor through `snd`. Mumford's hypothesis is that `X` is a complete
294	**variety** (irreducible) and `Y`, `Z` are **varieties**; the formal statement therefore needs
295	`X ⊗ Y` geometrically irreducible and reduced (so the dense-open rigidity glue applies) and `Z`
296	separated (so agreement on a dense open propagates). These three instances are added; they are
297	exactly what `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` (the gluing step) consumes, and what Milne's Rigidity
298	Theorem 1.1 lists ("`V` complete, `V × W` geometrically irreducible, `Z` separated").
299	
300	## iter-157 prover progress
301	
302	The categorical *skeleton* is fully discharged here; only the single geometric core
303	remains. Concretely, Mumford "chooses any point `x₀ ∈ X`" and sets `g(y) = f(x₀, y)`. We
304	make this explicit by adding a `k̄`-point `x₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ X` to the hypotheses (Mumford's
305	"complete variety `X`" is nonempty, so over `k̄` such a point exists; the downstream
306	consumer `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` supplies it from `ℙ¹`). The witness is then
307	
308	  `g := lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) ≫ f`   (the section `y ↦ (x₀, y)` post-composed with `f`).
309	
310	The goal `f = snd X Y ≫ g` rewrites — by pure cartesian-monoidal algebra (`comp_lift`,
311	`toUnit` uniqueness, `Category.comp_id`) — to
312	
313	  `f = retract ≫ f`,   where   `retract := lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`
314	
315	is the endomorphism `(x, y) ↦ (x₀, y)` of `X ⊗ Y` that collapses the `X`-axis onto `x₀`.
316	This reduction (lemma `rigidity_snd_lift`, then one `rw`) is closed below. The
317	remaining `f = retract ≫ f` is the genuine geometric heart (`rigidity_core`): `f` depends
318	only on the `Y`-coordinate. Its proof is Mumford's properness/closed-map/affine-constant
319	argument and is the one honestly-deferred obligation — see `rigidity_core` for the full
320	decomposition into the two Mathlib bridges still to be built.
321	
322	**Status**: iter-157 — categorical reduction closed; geometric core (`rigidity_core`) is
323	the sole remaining `sorry`. -/
324	theorem rigidity_lemma
325	    {X Y Z : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
326	    [IsProper X.hom]
327	    [GeometricallyIrreducible (X ⊗ Y).hom]
328	    [IsReduced (X ⊗ Y).left]
329	    [IsSeparated Z.hom]
330	    (f : (X ⊗ Y) ⟶ Z)
331	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X)
332	    (y₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Y)
333	    (z₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Z)
334	    (_hf : lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀) :
335	    ∃ g : Y ⟶ Z, f = snd X Y ≫ g := by
336	  -- Mumford's witness: `g(y) = f(x₀, y)`, i.e. restrict `f` to the slice `{x₀} × Y`.
337	  refine ⟨lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) ≫ f, ?_⟩
338	  -- Reassociate and collapse the projection-then-section composite.
339	  rw [← Category.assoc, rigidity_snd_lift]
340	  -- Goal is now the geometric core `f = retract ≫ f`.
341	  exact rigidity_core f x₀ y₀ z₀ _hf

8	/-! # The Jacobian of a smooth proper curve
9	
10	The Jacobian of a smooth, proper, geometrically irreducible curve over a field, equipped with
11	its structure as an abelian variety.
12	
13	## Status (Phase C scaffolding)
14	
15	The Jacobian is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of an Albanese witness
16	(`JacobianWitness`) for the curve `C`; the four protected instances on `Jacobian C` all
17	project from the witness directly.
18	
19	This file currently contains TWO `sorry`-bodied declarations (the Phase-C scaffolding
20	inventory):
21	
22	1. `genusZeroWitness` (iter-127 scaffold): a `JacobianWitness C` for a genus-`0` curve,
23	   with underlying scheme `Spec k`. The body is a `sorry` to be closed once pieces
24	   (i)+(ii)+(iii) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile land (iter-138+); the
25	   substantive mathematical content is the genus-`0` rigidity statement
26	   `rigidity_over_kbar` (M2.a; `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar`).
27	2. `nonempty_jacobianWitness` (Phase-C OFF-LIMITS sorry): existence of an Albanese
28	   witness for an arbitrary smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve; gated on
29	   M2 + M3 closure (Albanese existence in genus `≥ 1` plus genus-`0` rigidity).
30	
31	The file contains:
32	- `IsAlbanese`: the Albanese universal property for a pointed curve.
33	- `IsAlbanese.unique`: uniqueness of the Albanese object up to canonical isomorphism.
34	- `JacobianWitness`: a bundled candidate Albanese object together with the universal
35	  property uniformly over the choice of $k$-rational marked point.
36	- `genusZeroWitness`: an Albanese witness for a genus-`0` curve, body `sorry`
37	  (iter-127 scaffold; closure deferred to iter-138+).
38	- `nonempty_jacobianWitness`: existence of an Albanese witness for every curve —
39	  the headline Phase-C sorry, absorbing both higher-genus Albanese existence and
40	  genus-`0` rigidity.
41	- `Jacobian` and its four protected instances, each obtained by projection from the
42	  Albanese witness.
43	
44	### Forbidden shortcut (sanity check)
45	
46	Defining `Jacobian C := 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k)))` *unconditionally* (the terminal object,
47	i.e. `Spec k`) compiles and discharges three of the four instances for free, but the
48	fourth instance `SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C) (𝟙_ _).hom` would force `genus C = 0`,
49	which is mathematically wrong for any curve of genus `≥ 1`. The terminal-object definition
50	is therefore forbidden when `genus C > 0`. The witness-based definition avoids this
51	issue: in genus `0` the witness's underlying scheme `J` is `Spec k`, and in genus `> 0`
52	the witness's `J` is the honest Albanese object.
53	-/

61	namespace AlgebraicGeometry

63	variable {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
64	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
65	  [IsProper C.hom]
66	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]

68	/-- The Albanese universal property for a pointed smooth proper curve `(C, P)`.
69	An object `J` (with abelian-variety structure) is the Albanese of `(C, P)` if it
70	receives a universal pointed morphism `C ⟶ J` sending `P` to the identity. -/
71	def IsAlbanese (C : Over (Spec (.of k))) (P : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k))) ⟶ C)
72	    (J : Over (Spec (.of k)))
73	    [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] : Prop :=
74	  ∃ α : C ⟶ J, P ≫ α = η[J] ∧
75	    ∀ {A : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom]
76	      [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] (f : C ⟶ A) (_ : P ≫ f = η[A]),
77	      ∃! (g : J ⟶ A), f = α ≫ g
78	
79	namespace IsAlbanese
80	
81	noncomputable def ofCurve {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
82	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
83	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J) : C ⟶ J :=
84	  Classical.choose h
85	
86	lemma comp_ofCurve {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
87	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
88	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J) :
89	    P ≫ h.ofCurve = η[J] :=
90	  (Classical.choose_spec h).1
91	
92	lemma exists_unique_ofCurve_comp {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))}
93	    {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C} {J : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj J] [IsProper J.hom] [Smooth J.hom]
94	    [GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom] (h : IsAlbanese C P J)
95	    {A : Over (Spec (.of k))} [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom]
96	    [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] (f : C ⟶ A) (hf : P ≫ f = η[A]) :
97	    ∃! (g : J ⟶ A), f = h.ofCurve ≫ g :=
98	  (Classical.choose_spec h).2 f hf

100	/-- The Albanese object is unique up to a unique isomorphism compatible with the
101	universal morphisms. -/
102	theorem unique {k : Type u} [Field k] {C : Over (Spec (.of k))} {P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C}
103	    {J₁ J₂ : Over (Spec (.of k))}
104	    [GrpObj J₁] [IsProper J₁.hom] [Smooth J₁.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J₁.hom]
105	    [GrpObj J₂] [IsProper J₂.hom] [Smooth J₂.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible J₂.hom]
106	    (h₁ : IsAlbanese C P J₁) (h₂ : IsAlbanese C P J₂) :
107	    ∃! (e : J₁ ⟶ J₂), h₂.ofCurve = h₁.ofCurve ≫ e := by
108	  have ⟨g, hg_eq, hg_unique⟩ := h₁.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₂.ofCurve h₂.comp_ofCurve
109	  have ⟨h, hh_eq, hh_unique⟩ := h₂.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₁.ofCurve h₁.comp_ofCurve
110	  have ⟨g₁, hg₁_eq, hg₁_unique⟩ := h₁.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₁.ofCurve h₁.comp_ofCurve
111	  have g₁_eq_id : g₁ = 𝟙 J₁ := by
112	    refine (hg₁_unique (𝟙 J₁) ?_).symm
113	    simp
114	  have ggh_eq_g₁ : g ≫ h = g₁ := by
115	    refine hg₁_unique (g ≫ h) ?_
116	    change h₁.ofCurve = h₁.ofCurve ≫ (g ≫ h)
117	    rw [← Category.assoc, ← hg_eq, ← hh_eq]
118	  have hgh : g ≫ h = 𝟙 J₁ := by rw [ggh_eq_g₁, g₁_eq_id]
119	  have ⟨k₂, hk₂_eq, hk₂_unique⟩ := h₂.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp h₂.ofCurve h₂.comp_ofCurve
120	  have k₂_eq_id : k₂ = 𝟙 J₂ := by
121	    refine (hk₂_unique (𝟙 J₂) ?_).symm
122	    simp
123	  have hhg_eq_k₂ : h ≫ g = k₂ := by
124	    refine hk₂_unique (h ≫ g) ?_
125	    change h₂.ofCurve = h₂.ofCurve ≫ (h ≫ g)
126	    rw [← Category.assoc, ← hh_eq, ← hg_eq]
127	  have hhg : h ≫ g = 𝟙 J₂ := by rw [hhg_eq_k₂, k₂_eq_id]
128	  exact ⟨g, hg_eq, fun g' hg' => hg_unique g' hg'⟩
129	
130	end IsAlbanese

142	/-- A bundled Albanese witness for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
143	`C`: a candidate group scheme `J` (with proper, smooth, geometrically irreducible
144	structure, smooth of relative dimension `genus C`), and a proof that `J` satisfies
145	the Albanese universal property of Definition `IsAlbanese` for `C` *for every choice

148	Mathematically, the underlying scheme `J` (and its group/proper/smooth/irreducible
149	structure) is intrinsic to `C`; only the universal morphism `C ⟶ J` depends on the

152	`AbelJacobi.Jacobian.ofCurve P` needs in order to project the Albanese morphism for

156	which is the form required by `Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus`. -/
157	structure JacobianWitness (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
158	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] where
159	  /-- The candidate Albanese object. -/

161	  /-- The group-scheme structure on `J`. -/
162	  grpObj : GrpObj J
163	  /-- `J` is proper over `k`. -/
164	  proper : IsProper J.hom
165	  /-- `J` is smooth over `k`. -/
166	  smooth : Smooth J.hom
167	  /-- `J` is geometrically irreducible over `k`. -/
168	  geomIrred : GeometricallyIrreducible J.hom
169	  /-- `J` is smooth of relative dimension `genus C` (so dim `J = genus C`). -/
170	  smoothGenus : SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C) J.hom
171	  /-- The Albanese universal-property data: for every choice of $k$-rational marked
172	  point `P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`, the scheme `J` together with its group/proper/smooth/irreducible
173	  structure satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve `(C, P)`. -/
174	  isAlbaneseFor : ∀ (P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C), @IsAlbanese k _ C P J grpObj proper smooth geomIrred

211	    (h : genus C = 0) :

216	  -- supply each instance for the identity and let term-elaboration close by defeq.
217	  proper := (inferInstance : IsProper (𝟙 (Spec (.of k))))
218	  smooth := (inferInstance : Smooth (𝟙 (Spec (.of k))))
219	  geomIrred := geometricallyIrreducible_id_Spec k

221	    rw [h]; exact (inferInstance : SmoothOfRelativeDimension 0 (𝟙 (Spec (.of k))))
222	  isAlbaneseFor := by
223	    -- For every `k`-rational marked point `P`, exhibit the Albanese datum for
224	    -- the pointed curve `(C, P)` with `J = 𝟙_` (blueprint C.2 / C.3).

229	      -- equal by uniqueness of morphisms into the terminal object.
